summary: Fiery Rose solo and soaring Dew anchor a high‑energy Giants set
blurb: The show bursts with a blistering "Ramble On Rose" guitar showdown and an uplifting second‑set opener on "Morning Dew." Standout moments include the tight Drums/Space → The Other One transition and several inspired Dylan‑Dead collaborations.
review: July 12, 1987 at Giants Stadium captures the Dead in top form. Jerry Garcia lights up the stage with a fiery solo on "Ramble On Rose" that fans describe as “on fire,” while his pedal‑steel touches add a rare flavor to the set. The second set kicks off with a powerful "Morning Dew" that rides a wave of energy through the crowd, leading into a seamless Drums/Space → "The Other One" sequence that feels both tight and exploratory. Throughout the night the band delivers a strong Dylan‑Dead partnership – "Tomorrow Is A Long Time," "Chimes Of Freedom," and "John Brown" all receive glowing praise for their passionate execution. Brent’s keyboards, though sometimes buried in the mix, provide memorable fills and shine on the collaborative numbers. Phil’s bass stays solid and audible even on modest speakers, while Mickey and Bill lock into a tight rhythmic foundation that drives the jams forward. Overall, this gig is remembered as one of the high‑points of the 1987 tour, offering both raw power and nuanced musical moments.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings (5) give positive sentiment, highlighting standout songs and overall show excellence; only one mixed and one negative note focus on recording quality, not performance. Consistent praise across sources earns a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent matrix mix with crystal‑clear high end, balanced crowd ambience and no artifacts – captures the full dynamics of the performance.

key_highlights: Jerry’s incendiary "Ramble On Rose" solo and pedal‑steel moments
"Morning Dew" opens set two with soaring intensity
Tight Drums/Space → The Other One transition that drives the jam forward
Dylan‑Dead collaborations – "Tomorrow Is A Long Time," "Chimes Of Freedom," and "John Brown" – praised for passion and cohesion

band_performance.Jerry: Fiery solo on "Ramble On Rose," soaring lead work, pedal‑steel touches and magnetic stage presence drive the show.
band_performance.Phil: Solid, tight bass that remains audible even on modest speakers; clear in the excellent matrix mix.
band_performance.Bob: Engaged rhythm guitar and vocals; occasional vocal flubs but overall strong contribution throughout the set.
band_performance.Brent: Keyboard fills sometimes lost in the mix yet provide memorable moments; praised for passionate playing on Dylan‑Dead numbers like "Tomorrow Is A Long Time" and "Chimes Of Freedom."
band_performance.Mickey: Tight drumming, occasionally quiet but consistently supportive; key to the powerful Drums/Space → The Other One segment.
band_performance.Billy: Provides a solid backbone alongside Mickey; overall drum work contributes to the show's tight rhythmic drive.
